Secrets of the Kernel Debugger

By Forrest Trepte, Developer Support Engineer, Xbox Advanced Technology Group

The Microsoft® Visual C++® environment provides a powerful and easy-to-use debugger, which is the tool we recommend for debugging your game. The Xbox® SDK also provides a command-line debugger known as the Kernel Debugger (KD) that can be handy from time to time. The Xbox System Software team relied on the capabilities of KD to develop and debug the Xbox kernel1 and title libraries. KD can also be used to provide certain low-level information that cannot be accessed with the Visual C++ debugger.

KD offers the following unique capabilities:

  • At some points in the execution of the Xbox kernel, Visual C++ will be unable to break into the debugger or won't be able to show a call stack. However, KD shows information deep within the system software, and the debugger will be able to show you this information so that you can identify the call that is causing the problem.
  • Visual C++ can lose data if too many debug output statements occur simultaneously. In some cases, the debug versions of the XTL libraries detect errors or attempt to print warnings at times when communication with Visual C++ is impossible. On the other hand, KD catches all debug output messages and makes them available to you, thus helping track down potential sources of instability.
  • If your code runs in deferred procedure calls (DPCs), it cannot be debugged with Visual C++, but can be debugged with KD. Such code includes certain Microsoft® Direct3D® callbacks and debug channel extensions.
  • The command line nature of the Kernel Debugger makes it easier to share a debugging session with a remote location or to save a complete log of debugging activity.
  • KD offers some features that are not provided in the Visual C++ debugger, such as memory use reports.

KD can be complicated to learn, but this guide should get you up and running.

Setting up KD

You will need an Xbox Development Kit (XDK) and a null-modem cable to set up the Kernel Debugger. While Visual C++ uses the Ethernet port for debugging, all debugging with KD occurs via a serial port connection from your PC to the XDK.

Note   Green debug kits cannot be debugged with the Kernel Debugger because they do not have serial ports.

  1. Turn off your Xbox.
  2. Connect the null-modem cable between the serial port on the back of your Xbox and a spare COM port on your PC.
  3. Open a command prompt with the following environment variables: 
       _NT_DEBUG_PORT=COM1
   _NT_DEBUG_BAUD_RATE=115200
   _NT_SYMBOL_PATH=C:\Program Files\Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\symbols\4039

    Note   Be sure to adjust the above variables if you are using a different COM port or have installed the Xbox SDK at a different location. Also, "4039" at the end relates to the recovery disc version that was run on your Xbox. The number you should use is shown at the bottom-right corner of the XDK Launcher. If you used the recovery disc matching the month of the XDK that you installed on your PC, the symbols will be in a folder called Latest.2

    Most likely you'll want to set the above variables permanently for the machine rather than setting them every time you open a command prompt.

  4. Run I386KD from the command line on your development machine. The Kernel Debugger is installed in Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\bin\kd. Pass -lines to show source files and line numbers. The result should look like this: 
    C:\>"C:\Program Files\Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\bin\kd\i386kd" -lines
       Opened \\.\COM1
       Baud rate set to 115200
   
       Microsoft ® Windows ® Kernel Debugger
       Version 1.0.0006.0
       Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp. 1981-2000
   
       Waiting to reconnect...
  5. Press CTRL+C to start the debugging connection and to make sure things are working (this command is used in KD to break into the debugger). The result will look something like this: 
       Send Break in ...
   Connected to Windows 2000 4039 x86 compatible target, ptr64 FALSE
   Kernel Debugger connection established.
   Loaded kdextx86 extension DLL
   Loaded dbghelp extension DLL
   Symbol search path is: C:\Program Files\Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\symbols\
     4039;C:\YourGame\Debug
   Executable search path is:
   Xbox Kernel Version 4039 UP Checked x86 compatible
   Kernel base = 0x80010000 PsLoadedModuleList = 0x8004c188
   Break instruction exception - code 80000003 (first chance)
   ****************************************************************************
   *                                                                          *
   *   You are seeing this message because you pressed either                 *
   *       CTRL+C (if you run kd.exe) or                                      *
   *       CTRL+BREAK (if you run WinDBG)                                     *
   *   on your debugger machine's keyboard.                                   *
   *                    THIS IS NOT A BUG OR A SYSTEM CRASH                   *
   * If you did not intend to break into the debugger, press G, then          *
   * press ENTER now. This message might immediately reappear. If it          *
   * does, press G and ENTER again.                                           *
   *                                                                          *
   ****************************************************************************
   nt!RtlpBreakWithStatusInstruction:
   8002f880 cc               int     3
   kd>

You now know how to run KD and have verified that the connection between your PC and Xbox is working.

Debugging Your Game

  1. Place a call to the DebugBreak() function at the beginning of your program. This call ensures that your program will wait for the debugger to connect before running.
  2. Make sure that your game build generates a .pdb file so that you will have its debugging symbols.

    By default, the .pdb file is set up for debug builds of your project but not for the release configuration. Use the following project settings to ensure that your project generates a .pdb file (allowing you to debug your release builds in VC or KD):

    • C/C++, General, Debug info: Program Database
    • Link, General, Generate debug info: check the box
  3. Add the directory containing your game's .pdb file to the symbol path, as shown below: 
    _NT_SYMBOL_PATH=C:\Program Files\Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\symbols\4039;
C:\YourGame\Debug

    Your paths may vary depending on:

    • XDK installation directory (e.g., Microsoft Xbox SDK)
    • Recovery disk version number (e.g., 4039)
    • The configuration of your game and where it is built (e.g., C:\YourGame\Debug)

    If, instead of function names, you only see addresses like nt+0x1f878 or YourGame+0x306, the files on your symbol path are either missing or do not match the version on your Xbox.

  4. Run KD.

    If KD is already running, let it continue to run. There is no need to exit and restart KD when you reboot your Xbox.

  5. Boot your Xbox and launch your title.
  6. Press CTRL+C to break into the debugger.

    You will see the following:

       nt!Rt1pBreakWithStatusInstruction:
   8002f880 cc               int     3
  7. To continue execution, type the G command. (Press G, followed by ENTER.)

    The program will stop at the DebugBreak() function you placed in your code. You will see the following:

       Break instruction exception - code 80000003 (first chance)
   nt!DbgBreakPoint:
   8002f878 cc               int     3
  8. Type .reload.

    The .reload command instructs the Kernel Debugger to find the debug symbols for whatever the debugger is currently executing. This search will produce a result similar to the following:

       Connected to Windows 2000 4039 x86 compatible target, ptr64 FALSE
   Loading Kernel Symbols
   .........
   Loading unloaded module list
   Unable to examine unloaded module list
   Loading User Symbols
   UserMode Module List Address is NULL (Addr= 8004cba4)
   This is usually caused by being in the wrong process
   context or by paging.
  9. To examine the call stack, type the K command.

    The kernel's startup function, your game's main routine, and the call to the break function will appear:

    ChildEBP RetAddr
d0032d60 0001112a nt!DbgBreakPoint [..\i386\debug2.asm @ 42]
d0032d6c 000119a0 YourGame!main+0x9 [c:\your game\testkd.cpp @ 63]
d0032d80 00012bcf YourGame!mainXapiStartup+0x5c [d:\xbox\private\ntos\xapi\dll\
  xapi0.c @ 52]
d0032dcc 8002810c YourGame!XapiThreadStartup+0xfd [d:\xbox\private\ntos\xapi\
  k32\thread.c @ 193]
00000000 00000000 nt!KiThreadStartup+0x20 [..\i386\threadbg.asm @ 94]

    If, instead of function names such as nt!DbgBreakPoint or YourGame!main, addresses like nt+0x1f878 or YourGame+0x306 appear, either: (1) your symbol search path is set incorrectly, or (2) the .pdb file does not match your game's .xbe.

  10. Type the G command.

    Your program will continue to run.

You now have the basics for running and debugging your game. Any debug trace statements produced while the game runs will appear in KD window. To break into the debugger, press CTRL+C. The Kernel Debugger will also stop in response to a DebugBreak() call (int 3), XTL library error, or a crash.

Capturing a Crash Report

There are commonly used commands that provide information about where a problem is occurring. If you encounter a bug that is difficult to reproduce it is always preferable to hold it in the debugger until it can be completely investigated. However, sometimes it is necessary to reboot the machine to continue work. If so, you should first save a log containing debug output that preceded the crash as well as the result of typing the following commands:

r

List register values.

  

kp

Call stack, including parameters. Note that the line numbers displayed are generally for the line following the current instruction. (The kp command is only available in the version of the Kernel Debugger included in the February 2002 XDK and later. If the kp command does not work, use the kb command instead.)

  

dd esp L80

Dump raw stack data. Print out memory as DWORDs beginning at esp, the stack pointer. L80 stands for length 80 hex, which requests 128 DWORDs of data. These values can be used to see local variables or other data on the stack if you know how the stack is arranged.

  

ln eip
u xxxxxxxx xxxxxxxx

Disassemble the function that contains the crash. Here, the ln command lists the symbols near the current instruction pointer (eip)—the code where the crash occurred. The output will look something like the following: 

  (00011050)  YourGame!main+0x9  |
  (0001109d)  YourGame!DebugBreak.Next

Using the two addresses that the ln command printed in parentheses, type the u command. In this example the second command would be u 00011050 0001109d.

  

!process 0 7

Print information about the process and print a call stack for each thread.

  

dd nt!KeTickCount L1

Print the number of milliseconds the box was running before it crashed (without including the time spent paused in the debugger). This command prints the value of the kernelis tick count variable. To determine the number of seconds the box has been running since the last reboot, convert from hex to decimal and then divide the result by 1000 (divide the result by 3,600,000 to determine the number of hours).

Other Useful Commands

CTRL+B

Exit KD.

  

<ENTER>

Repeat previous command.

  

?

Print list of basic commands.

  

.help

List "." meta-commands.

  

!help

List "!" debugger extensions.

  

x MODULE!SYMBOL

Examine symbols. Use with wildcards such as * to list all symbols in a module. For example, x YourGame!x* (where YourGame is the name of your program) will list all of the symbols beginning with "x" that reside in your program, including the symbols of functions linked into your program from the XTL libraries. When searching for symbols, two underscores are used to represent a class method. Thus, MyClass::MyMethod would be shown as MyClass__MyMethod.

  

db ADDRESS

Dump bytes at memory address. Also shows ASCII characters. (Follow with repeated db commands to list more.)

  

dd ADDRESS

Dump DWORDs at memory address.

  

df ADDRESS

Dump floats at memory address.

  

dds ADDRESS

Dump DWORDs with symbols. If a DWORD value appears to match a symbol, the symbol name (typically a function name) will be listed as well. This is mainly useful as dds esp to show the exact data on the stack along with symbols matching return addresses. This can also be useful if nonstandard function calling conventions confuse the regular stack trace commands.

  

dt ADDRESS

Display type. This handy command prints out the values for each field of a structure in a nice easy-to-read list. The switch -b can be used to show substructures by recursively traversing into fields that are themselves structures. Also, it is possible to give the name of a type followed by an address. For example, "dt CXBoxSample 0xd003267c" would show the address 0xd003267c cast as a CXBoxSample class.

  

rM 54

Display floating-point, MMX, and SIMD XMM registers. (Note that "M" must be upper case.)

  

rM 1fd

Display every CPU register.

  

p

Program step. Executes one instruction, stepping over calls or interrupts.

  

t

Trace. Executes one instruction, stepping into calls or interrupts.

  

bp ADDRESS

Set breakpoint at instruction address.

  

bp ADDRESS "commands"

KD provides a kind of automated breakpoint that can sometimes be handy while debugging. This allows you to run a list of commands each time a breakpoint is hit. Commands are separated by semicolons and frequently you may want to make the last one the Go command. This allows you to print out a log of information, where each time you hit the breakpoint you run a couple commands and then continue running. For example, if you enter a breakpoint like this:

	bp CXBMesh::Render "kp;g"

then as you run KD will print out the call stack every time the CXBMesh::Render() function is hit, allowing to see all the cases where meshes are being rendered.

  

ba w1 ADDRESS

Break on access. Sets a data breakpoint that will fire on execution of an instruction that writes to the byte at ADDRESS. This command is useful if you know a piece of memory is being overwritten and you want to track down the responsible code. This command can also be used with "r" instead of "w" to break on a memory read. In addition, "2" or "4" can be used instead of "1" to break on access to a WORD or DWORD instead of a single byte.

  

bl

Breakpoint list. Lists current breakpoints.

  

bc

Breakpoint clear. Removes the breakpoint with an index number shown in the leftmost column of the bl command output.

  

!memusage

Print summary of free memory pages and free memory use by category.

  

!heap 0 –a

Print general information, and print each heap entry for all heaps. This can take a very long time and dump a ton of output. The result reflects the internal heap data structures—the addresses shown will be located before the actual memory address of the allocated data, and the size of the block can be significantly larger than the size your program requested. If you become familiar with this information, it may help you understand your program's allocation patterns and also help you spot memory leaks. When an allocation is freed, its entry no longer says "busy" until that memory spot is reclaimed by a new allocation. You can use heap entries to study how a heap becomes fragmented.

  

!handle 0 7

Print information about all handles on the system. This can be useful for tracking open files and other handle-based resources on the system.

  

!poolused 3

Print report of pool memory allocated by the system.

  

!poolfind TAG

List all the pool allocations of a particular type, where TAG is one of the case-sensitive four-character categories listed by !poolused.

  

.reboot

Reboot the Xbox.

  

.sympath

Show current symbol search path.

  

.sympath PATH

Set symbol search path to specific path. This action is not needed if the _NT_SYMBOL_PATH environment variable was set before running KD. But if you use the Kernel Debugger and the symbol path is incorrect, you can change the path and then run .reload to get back on track.

  

.echotimestamps

Toggle on and off display of the current time along with debug output messages and debugger breaks. This action can help you understand the timing of a sequence of events.

Tips and Tricks

Getting a Call Stack After Breaking in with CTRL+C

When you press CTRL+C in KD, execution will halt the next time a clock interrupt occurs. You can use this interrupt to find out what code is executing when your game has not crashed but is not proceeding as it should, such when an infinite loop or deadlock occurs. However, the output you receive from the k command will not be very interesting, listing the interrupt function instead of your program's functions. When this occurs—actually whenever you see nt!KeClockInterrupt on the stack—you can use a special technique to view the stack as it existed right before the interrupt occurred. Type the kv command and then look for TrapFrame ? ADDRESS. Next, run !trap ADDRESS followed by !kb to see the stack. (The kv command displays a stack with interrupt trap frame information listed. !trap sets the register context to a trap frame, and !kb displays a stack trace for that frame.)

Here is an example:

kd> kb
ChildEBP RetAddr Args to Child
d0032d08 80042e29 00000001 00000000 d0032d60 nt!RtlpBreakWithStatusInstruction 
 [..\i386\debug2.asm @ 53]
d0032d08 00000000 00000001 00000000
d0032d60 nt!KeClockInterrupt+0x121 [..\i386\clockint.asm @ 243]
fffffffc 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 0x0
kd> kv
ChildEBP RetAddr Args to Child
d0032d08 80042e29 00000001 00000000 d0032d60 nt!RtlpBreakWithStatusInstruction 
 (FPO: [1,0,0]) [..\i386\debug2.asm @ 53]
d0032d08 00000000 00000001 00000000 d0032d60 nt!KeClockInterrupt+0x121 (
  FPO: [0,2] TrapFrame @ d0032d18) [..\i386\clockint.asm @ 243]
fffffffc 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 0x0
kd> !trap d0032d18
eax=00000001 ebx=d0032de0 ecx=000000c0 edx=00000000 esi=00000000 edi=d0032dec
eip=00011003 esp=d0032d60 ebp=d0032d60 iopl=0         nv up ei pl nz na pe nc
vip=0    vif=0
cs=0008  ss=0010                                                 efl=00000202
ErrCode = d0032d54
00011003 b801000000       mov     eax,0x1

kd> !kb
ChildEBP RetAddr  Args to Child
d0032d60 00011171 00000005 d0032dcc 000119a0 yourgame!InfiniteLoop+0x3
d0032d6c 000119a0 00000000 00000000 00000000 yourgame!main+0x50
d0032d80 00012bcf 00000000 00000000 00000000 yourgame!mainXapiStartup+0x5c
d0032dcc 8002810c 00011944 00000000 d0032de0 yourgame!XapiThreadStartup+0xfd
00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 nt!KiThreadStartup+0x20

Connecting to a Console That's Already Dead

You should always try to run tests while attached to a debugger. However, occasionally someone will hit that difficult-to-reproduce bug that you need to investigate while their Xbox is disconnected. You will miss all the debug output preceding the crash, but it is still possible at this point to connect the null-modem cable from the debug kit to your PC, and then run KD to retrieve a call stack or run other debugging commands. To get to the crash point, when KD says "Waiting to reconnect…", you may need to press CTRL+C. When KD output produces nt!RtlpBreakWithStatusInstruction, type the G command, and you will be at the crash site where you can investigate what caused the error.

Remote Access to a Debugging Session

You may want to develop the habit of running remote.exe, which is also located in your Microsoft Xbox SDK\xbox\bin\kd directory. If that directory is located on your path, you can launch the kernel debugger with remote as follows:

remote /S "i386kd -lines" SessionName

SessionName can be any name that you'd like. You would need to use different names if you have multiple debuggers connected to a single PC, as would typically be done in a testing lab with racks of Xbox consoles to be debugged. You can proceed to use the Kernel Debugger as you normally would, but if you want to work with someone working on another computer on your local network, they can type the following command:

remote /C DEBUGGER_COMPUTER_NAME SessionName

where DEBUGGER_COMPUTER_NAME is the name of the PC to connect to.

When running remote /S to start a debugging session, the title bar of your command prompt will show the remote /C command others will use to connect to your session. If you forget the session name, you can always check the title bar.

When two or more people are connected to a debugging session via remote clients, they can each type commands and view the results. To quit without affecting others that are connected to the remote client, individuals can either type @q or simply close their command window.

For More Information

Documentation about the Kernel Debugger is included with the Microsoft® Debugging Tools, which can be downloaded at http://www.microsoft.com/whdc/ddk/debugging/. This documentation is a useful reference that includes all KD extensions and commands. The most important commands are supported on Xbox, but you should be aware that some KD commands work only for debugging Windows software.

1 The kernel resides in ROM on every console and controls fundamental services such as boot-up, hardware access, virtual memory, and thread execution. For more information about the Xbox kernel, see Xbox Kernel Overview.

2 In XDK releases before February 2002, there isn't a symbols directory for every recovery disc version when recovery discs have the same kernel, and there is no Latest folder. If there isn't a folder matching your version number, use the highest previous number that does have a directory.

Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
